blocks.cap: Two cables, one squaring trick. Cable A is \(6\sqrt{7}\) and Cable B is \(4\sqrt{15}\), and comparing those roots head on is hard. Square each instead. Cable A becomes \(6^2 \times 7 = 36 \times 7 = 252\) and Cable B becomes \(4^2 \times 15 = 16 \times 15 = 240\). Since both cables are nonnegative, squaring keeps their order, so \(252 > 240\) tells us \(6\sqrt{7} > 4\sqrt{15}\) and Cable A wins.

blocks.x: Here is the move we found, dressed up so it works every time. Since two nonnegative quantities keep their order when I square them, whichever one squares to the bigger number is the bigger quantity, so to compare any two root expressions I can just square both and rank the plain whole numbers I get instead. A number sitting in front of a root comes along for the ride, because it squares too, so something like \(6\sqrt{7}\) squares to \(6^2 \times 7 = 252\), turning an awkward irrational into one tidy number. The decimals I was reaching for were never needed, and I am only deciding which is larger here, not simplifying or combining the radicals.

Solving a geometry formula leaves you with the expression \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4\), and your teacher promises that even though \(\sqrt{6}\) is one of those messy roots that never settles down into a tidy decimal, this whole thing lands on a clean whole number. Here is why. The fourth power means four copies of \(\sqrt{6}\) multiplied together, which you can split into two pairs. Each pair \(\sqrt{6} \times \sqrt{6}\) gives back the number under the root, namely \(6\), because that is exactly what a square root does. So the two pairs turn into \(6 \times 6\). What whole number does \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4\) equal? Type the single whole number.
hints: A square root undoes a square. Multiply two copies of the same root together, \(\sqrt{6} \times \sqrt{6}\), and you are left with just the inside, \(6\). No decimals needed.

The fourth power is two of those pairs side by side, so \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4 = \left(\sqrt{6}\times\sqrt{6}\right)\times\left(\sqrt{6}\times\sqrt{6}\right) = 6 \times 6\). Now just multiply those two sixes.
blocks.sol: The trick is to never touch the decimal value of \(\sqrt{6}\) at all. Group the four copies into pairs. One pair squares the root exactly, \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^2 = 6\), since a square and a square root cancel for nonnegative numbers. The fourth power is just that result squared again, so \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4 = \left(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^2\right)^2 = 6^2\), and \(6^2 = 36\). You can sanity check this against the bracketing idea. We know \(2 < \sqrt{6} < 3\) because the perfect squares \(2^2 = 4\) and \(3^2 = 9\) trap \(6\) between them. Raising everything to the fourth power keeps the order, so \(2^4 < \left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4 < 3^4\), meaning the answer must land between \(16\) and \(81\). The exact value sits comfortably inside that window. So \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4 = \boxed{36}\).

blocks.x: Here is the surprise we just caught. A square root and the number under it are tied together by one promise, that the root times itself gives back the inside, so \(\sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{6} = 6\) even though \(\sqrt{6}\) by itself never settles into a tidy decimal. That means any even power of a root is secretly a stack of those pairs, and each pair quietly collapses to the inside, so the messy irrational vanishes and a clean whole number is left standing. The fourth power is the inside squared and the sixth power is the inside cubed, because splitting an even exponent into pairs is the same as raising the inside number to half of that exponent.

Two walking trails meet end to end, so a hiker covers both in a row. The first trail is \(\sqrt{18}\) miles long and the second is \(\sqrt{33}\) miles long. A planner wants to print the largest whole number of miles that the full route stays above, in other words the floor of the total length. Start loose by trapping each root between integers, then sharpen with tenths until the sum is pinned between two consecutive whole numbers. What is the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{18} + \sqrt{33}\)? Type the single whole number.
hints: Bracket each root by itself first. Since \(4^2 = 16\) and \(5^2 = 25\), \(\sqrt{18}\) sits between 4 and 5. Since \(5^2 = 25\) and \(6^2 = 36\), \(\sqrt{33}\) sits between 5 and 6. That only tells you the sum is between 9 and 11, which is too wide to name a single floor.

Push the lower edge up with tenths. Check \(4.2^2 = 17.64\), which is below 18, so \(\sqrt{18} > 4.2\). Check \(5.7^2 = 32.49\), which is below 33, so \(\sqrt{33} > 5.7\). Adding the safe lower edges gives more than \(4.2 + 5.7\). Now find a matching upper edge to trap the sum between two neighbors.
blocks.sol: The total length is \(\sqrt{18} + \sqrt{33}\), and I want the largest whole number below it. Squaring is the honest tool here, since for nonnegative numbers a bigger square means a bigger root. First a rough trap. From \(4^2 = 16 < 18 < 25 = 5^2\) I get \(4 < \sqrt{18} < 5\), and from \(5^2 = 25 < 33 < 36 = 6^2\) I get \(5 < \sqrt{33} < 6\). Together that says the sum is between \(9\) and \(11\), too loose to name one floor. Now sharpen with tenths, comparing exact squares each time. Since \(4.2^2 = 17.64 < 18\), I know \(\sqrt{18} > 4.2\). Since \(5.7^2 = 32.49 < 33\), I know \(\sqrt{33} > 5.7\). Adding these safe lower edges, \(\sqrt{18} + \sqrt{33} > 4.2 + 5.7 = 9.9\), which is already above \(9\). For the ceiling, \(4.25^2 = 18.0625 > 18\) gives \(\sqrt{18} < 4.25\), and \(5.75^2 = 33.0625 > 33\) gives \(\sqrt{33} < 5.75\), so the sum is less than \(4.25 + 5.75 = 10\). The total is squeezed into \(9.9 < \sqrt{18} + \sqrt{33} < 10\), comfortably between \(9\) and \(10\). The largest whole number below it is \(\boxed{9}\).

blocks.x: A survey drone leaves its base, flies \(\sqrt{40}\) miles north, then turns and flies \(\sqrt{75}\) miles east. The flight planner wants the largest whole number of miles that is still below the drone's total flown distance \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75}\). First trap each root. Since \(6^2 = 36\) and \(7^2 = 49\), we know \(\sqrt{40}\) sits between \(6\) and \(7\). Since \(8^2 = 64\) and \(9^2 = 81\), we know \(\sqrt{75}\) sits between \(8\) and \(9\). So the sum lands somewhere between \(14\) and \(16\), and it looks dangerously close to \(15\). The eyeball cannot settle this, so you will have to test \(15\) exactly. What is the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75}\)? Type the single whole number.
hints: The whole question is whether the sum slips below 15 or not. Rewrite the test \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75} < 15\) by moving one root across, so you are asking whether \(\sqrt{40} < 15 - \sqrt{75}\). Both sides are nonnegative, since \(\sqrt{75} < 9 < 15\), so you are allowed to square both sides without flipping the comparison.

Squaring \(\sqrt{40} < 15 - \sqrt{75}\) gives \(40 < 225 - 30\sqrt{75} + 75\), which tidies up to \(30\sqrt{75} < 260\). There is still one root left, so square one more time to land on plain whole numbers. Compare \(30^2 \cdot 75\) against \(260^2\).
blocks.sol: The sum clearly beats \(14\), since \(\sqrt{40} > 6\) and \(\sqrt{75} > 8\) force \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75} > 14\). The real contest is whether it stays under \(15\), so test \(15\) exactly. Ask whether \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75} < 15\). Move one root to the other side, so the question becomes whether \(\sqrt{40} < 15 - \sqrt{75}\). The right side is positive because \(\sqrt{75} < 9 < 15\), so both sides are nonnegative and squaring keeps the order. Squaring gives \(40 < 225 - 30\sqrt{75} + 75\), and the right side collects to \(300 - 30\sqrt{75}\). Rearranging, \(30\sqrt{75} < 260\). One root still remains, so square once more to reach plain whole numbers. The left side squares to \(30^2 \cdot 75 = 900 \cdot 75 = 67{,}500\), and the right side squares to \(260^2 = 67{,}600\). Since \(67{,}500 < 67{,}600\), the comparison \(30\sqrt{75} < 260\) is true, every step reverses cleanly, and the sum really is below \(15\). It already cleared \(14\), so the sum sits strictly between \(14\) and \(15\). The largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75}\) is \(\boxed{14}\).

review.x: A square sticker covers exactly \(34\) square centimeters, so each side measures \(\sqrt{34}\) centimeters. The number \(34\) is not a perfect square, so this side length cannot be written exactly, but you can trap it between two whole numbers using perfect square landmarks. What is the largest whole number that is less than \(\sqrt{34}\), in other words its floor? Type the single whole number.
hints: Look for the perfect square just below \(34\) and the one just above it. Think about which whole numbers, when squared, land on either side.
Since \(5^2 = 25\) and \(6^2 = 36\), the value \(34\) sits between them, so \(\sqrt{34}\) sits between \(5\) and \(6\). The floor is the lower of those two.
review.sol: To find the floor of \(\sqrt{34}\), hunt for the consecutive whole numbers whose squares bracket \(34\). Squaring \(5\) gives \(5^2 = 25\), which is below \(34\). Squaring \(6\) gives \(6^2 = 36\), which is above \(34\). So \(25 < 34 < 36\), and because a larger nonnegative number always has a larger square root, taking the root of each part keeps the order, giving \(5 < \sqrt{34} < 6\). The root lands strictly between \(5\) and \(6\), so the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{34}\) is \(\boxed{5}\).

review.x: A climber lays out two ropes side by side. Rope 1 is marked \(\sqrt{63}\) meters and Rope 2 is marked \(\sqrt{57}\) meters. Both lengths land between the same two whole numbers, so just looking at the floors will not settle it. Using the order rule for nonnegative numbers, which rope is longer? Option 1 is \(\sqrt{63}\). Option 2 is \(\sqrt{57}\). Type 1 or 2.
hints: Both roots sit between 7 and 8, since \(7^2 = 49\) and \(8^2 = 64\). The floor is the same for both, so you cannot tell them apart that way. Look instead at what is under each root.
For nonnegative numbers, the larger number has the larger square root. Squaring \(\sqrt{63}\) gives \(63\) and squaring \(\sqrt{57}\) gives \(57\), so compare \(63\) and \(57\).
review.sol: Squaring a nonnegative number keeps the order, so to compare \(\sqrt{63}\) and \(\sqrt{57}\) I compare their squares. Squaring undoes each root, giving \(63\) and \(57\). Since \(63 > 57\), the root of \(63\) is the larger value, which means \(\sqrt{63} > \sqrt{57}\). Both roots round down to \(7\), so the floors tie, but the exact squares break the tie cleanly. Rope 1 is longer, so the answer is option \(\boxed{1}\).

review.x: A square window has an area of \(75\) square inches, so the length of one side is exactly \(\sqrt{75}\) inches. You want that side length to the nearest whole inch. Since \(8^2 = 64\) and \(9^2 = 81\), the root is trapped between \(8\) and \(9\). To round, look at the halfway mark \(8.5\), whose square is \(72.25\). Option 1 is that \(75\) is below \(72.25\), and option 2 is that \(75\) is above \(72.25\). To the nearest whole inch, what is \(\sqrt{75}\)? Type the single whole number.
hints: The two whole numbers in play are 8 and 9, since 8 squared is 64 and 9 squared is 81 and 75 lands between them. Rounding just asks which one 75 is closer to, and the halfway point is 8.5.

Instead of squaring 8.5 with a decimal, double everything and compare whole numbers. The side doubled is twice the root of 75, and its square is 4 times 75, which is 300. The halfway value doubled is 17, and 17 squared is 289. Whichever square is bigger tells you which way to round.
review.sol: The side of the window is \(\sqrt{75}\), and you want it to the nearest whole inch. First trap it between landmarks. Since \(8^2 = 64\) and \(9^2 = 81\), and \(75\) sits between \(64\) and \(81\), you know \(8 < \sqrt{75} < 9\). To round, you compare \(\sqrt{75}\) to the halfway value \(8.5\). Rather than work with the decimal square \(72.25\), keep everything as whole numbers by doubling. Doubling the root gives \(2\sqrt{75}\), whose square is \(4 \times 75 = 300\). Doubling \(8.5\) gives \(17\), whose square is \(289\). Since \(300 > 289\), the root \(\sqrt{75}\) is greater than \(8.5\), so it rounds up. That makes option 2 the true statement, and the side rounds to \(9\) inches. To the nearest whole inch, \(\sqrt{75} = \boxed{9}\).

review.x: A skateboard ramp climbs \(\sqrt{44}\) feet from the ground to its top edge, and the builder wants that height rounded to the nearest whole foot for the sign out front. Since \(6^2 = 36\) and \(7^2 = 49\), the height lands somewhere between 6 and 7 feet. The deciding line is the halfway mark 6.5, whose square is \(42.25\). Option 1 is that \(\sqrt{44}\) rounds down to 6, and option 2 is that it rounds up to 7. To the nearest whole foot, what is \(\sqrt{44}\)? Type the single whole number.
hints: First trap the root between two integers. Find the consecutive whole numbers whose squares sit just below and just above 44, and you will know which two feet the height falls between.
Now decide which one is closer using the midpoint 6.5. Compare 44 with \(6.5^2 = 42.25\). If 44 is the larger number, the root is past the halfway point and rounds up.
review.sol: Start by trapping the root between landmark perfect squares. Because \(6^2 = 36\) and \(7^2 = 49\), and 44 sits between 36 and 49, the order rule tells us \(6 < \sqrt{44} < 7\). So the height is somewhere in the 6 to 7 range, and rounding comes down to which integer is nearer. The tie breaker is the midpoint 6.5, and squaring it gives \(6.5^2 = 42.25\). Compare the number under the root with that midpoint square. Since \(44 \ge 42.25\), the value 44 sits above the halfway mark, which means \(\sqrt{44}\) is greater than 6.5 and therefore closer to 7 than to 6. To keep the comparison fully exact you can clear the decimal by doubling before squaring, since \(\sqrt{44}\) versus \(6.5\) is the same as \(2\sqrt{44}\) versus \(13\), and squaring both nonnegative sides gives \(4 \times 44 = 176\) against \(13^2 = 169\). Because \(176 \ge 169\), the root really is past the midpoint, confirming it rounds up. To the nearest whole foot the ramp height is \(\boxed{7}\).

review.x: A length of wire measures \(\sqrt{31}\) inches, and a label needs that length rounded to the nearest tenth. First trap it. Since \(5.5^2 = 30.25\) sits below \(31\) and \(5.6^2 = 31.36\) sits above \(31\), the value lies between \(5.5\) and \(5.6\). To decide which way it rounds, check the halfway value \(5.55\), whose square is \(30.8025\). Compare \(31\) against the two options, option 1 being \(30.8025\) and option 2 being \(31\) itself, to see whether \(\sqrt{31}\) is above or below \(5.55\). To the nearest tenth, what is \(\sqrt{31}\)? Type the one-decimal answer.
hints: You already trapped the root between \(5.5\) and \(5.6\). The only question left is which tenth it is closer to, so look at the midpoint \(5.55\).
Compare the squares instead of the roots. Since \(5.55^2 = 30.8025\) is less than \(31\), the root \(\sqrt{31}\) is bigger than \(5.55\), so it rounds up.
review.sol: Squaring keeps order for nonnegative numbers, so I can compare squares instead of roots. The consecutive tenths around the answer are \(5.5\) and \(5.6\). Their squares are \(5.5^2 = 30.25\) and \(5.6^2 = 31.36\). Since \(30.25 \le 31 \le 31.36\), the value \(\sqrt{31}\) lies between \(5.5\) and \(5.6\). To round, I test the halfway point \(5.55\), where \(5.55^2 = 30.8025\). Because \(30.8025 \le 31\), the root \(\sqrt{31}\) is greater than \(5.55\), which means it sits in the upper part of the interval and rounds up. To the nearest tenth, \(\sqrt{31} = \boxed{5.6}\).

review.x: A flagpole's height comes out to \(\sqrt{88}\) units, and the builder needs it rounded to the nearest tenth. First trap the root. Since \(9.3^2 = 86.49\) sits below \(88\) and \(9.4^2 = 88.36\) sits above \(88\), the root lands between \(9.3\) and \(9.4\). To decide which way it rounds, check the halfway value \(9.35\), whose square is \(87.4225\). Option 1 is that \(88\) is below \(87.4225\), and option 2 is that \(88\) is above \(87.4225\). Rounded to the nearest tenth, what is \(\sqrt{88}\)? Type the one-decimal answer.
hints: The root sits between 9.3 and 9.4 because their squares, 86.49 and 88.36, bracket 88. The tenths digit is decided by the halfway point 9.35.
Square the halfway value 9.35 to get 87.4225, then compare 88 against it. Since 88 is bigger, the root is past the halfway mark, so it rounds up.
review.sol: Start by trapping \(\sqrt{88}\) between two tenths. Squaring gives \(9.3^2 = 86.49\) and \(9.4^2 = 88.36\), and since \(86.49 \le 88 \le 88.36\), the root lies between \(9.3\) and \(9.4\). To round, compare \(88\) with the square of the midpoint \(9.35\). Working exactly, \(935^2 = 874225\) while \(88 \times 10000 = 880000\), and \(880000 > 874225\), so \(9.35^2 = 87.4225\) is less than \(88\). That means \(\sqrt{88}\) is larger than \(9.35\), past the halfway mark, so it rounds up to the next tenth. To the nearest tenth, \(\sqrt{88} \approx \boxed{9.4}\).

review.x: A square field has an area of \(250\) square yards, so each side measures \(\sqrt{250}\) yards. The side length sits between two whole numbers because \(15^2 = 225\) and \(16^2 = 256\), and \(225 \le 250 \le 256\). What is the largest whole number that is less than \(\sqrt{250}\)? Type the single whole number.
hints: Look for the perfect square just below \(250\). Since \(15^2 = 225\), the root of \(250\) is a little more than \(15\).
Confirm the upper landmark too. Because \(16^2 = 256\) is more than \(250\), the root has not yet reached \(16\), so it is trapped between \(15\) and \(16\).
review.sol: I want the largest whole number that is still smaller than \(\sqrt{250}\), so I trap the root between two consecutive integers by comparing exact squares. The perfect square just below \(250\) is \(15^2 = 225\), and the next one up is \(16^2 = 256\). Since \(225 \le 250 \le 256\) and a larger number always has a larger square root for nonnegative numbers, I get \(15 < \sqrt{250} < 16\). The root is bigger than \(15\) (because \(250\) is more than \(225\)) but it has not reached \(16\) yet (because \(250\) is less than \(256\)). So the largest whole number below \(\sqrt{250}\) is \(\boxed{15}\).

review.x: A square reservoir holds water across a footprint of exactly \(9000\) square meters. Since the footprint is a square, each side measures \(\sqrt{9000}\) meters, which is not a whole number. To label the edge with a clean marker, the engineers want the largest whole number that is still less than \(\sqrt{9000}\). What is that whole number?
hints: You are hunting for two consecutive whole numbers whose squares sit just below and just above \(9000\). The smaller of those two is your answer.
Try squaring numbers in the nineties. Check \(94 \times 94\) and \(95 \times 95\) and see which one slips past \(9000\).
review.sol: The largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{9000}\) is the floor of that root, so I look for the two consecutive whole numbers whose squares trap \(9000\). Testing nearby values, \(94^2 = 8836\) and \(95^2 = 9025\). Since \(8836 < 9000 < 9025\), squaring shows \(94 < \sqrt{9000} < 95\). Because squaring preserves order for nonnegative numbers, \(\sqrt{9000}\) lands strictly between \(94\) and \(95\), so the largest whole number below it is \(\boxed{94}\).

review.x: A digital odometer is rolling toward a reading of \(\sqrt{3{,}000{,}000}\) tics, and a checkpoint flags the largest whole number that stays below it. Since \(1{,}000^2 = 1{,}000{,}000\) and \(2{,}000^2 = 4{,}000{,}000\), the root lands somewhere in the seventeen-hundreds. What is the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{3{,}000{,}000}\)? Type the single whole number.
hints: You want the biggest whole number whose square is still at or below \(3{,}000{,}000\). Try a number in the seventeen-hundreds and square it, then nudge up or down.
Compare \(1{,}732^2\) against \(3{,}000{,}000\), then check \(1{,}733^2\). The answer is the larger one that does not pass \(3{,}000{,}000\).
review.sol: I want the largest whole number whose square stays at or below \(3{,}000{,}000\), because that whole number is the floor of \(\sqrt{3{,}000{,}000}\). Squaring keeps order for nonnegative numbers, so I compare exact squares instead of decimals. Testing \(1{,}732\) gives \(1{,}732^2 = 2{,}999{,}824\), which is at or below \(3{,}000{,}000\). Testing the next number up, \(1{,}733^2 = 3{,}003{,}289\), which passes \(3{,}000{,}000\). So \(1{,}732^2 \le 3{,}000{,}000 < 1{,}733^2\), which traps the root between \(1{,}732\) and \(1{,}733\). The largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{3{,}000{,}000}\) is \(\boxed{1732}\).

review.x: A number puzzle. How many whole numbers \(n\) have a square root that is strictly greater than \(\sqrt{50}\) and strictly less than \(\sqrt{130}\)? Type the single count.
hints: For nonnegative numbers, a bigger number always has a bigger square root, so you can turn this into a question about \(n\) itself by squaring both ends.
Squaring \(\sqrt{50}\) gives \(50\) and squaring \(\sqrt{130}\) gives \(130\), so you need the whole numbers \(n\) strictly between \(50\) and \(130\). Count them carefully, leaving both ends out.
review.sol: The order rule from this lesson says that for nonnegative numbers, the one with the larger value has the larger square root, and the comparison runs both ways. So \(\sqrt{50} < \sqrt{n}\) is the same as \(50 < n\), and \(\sqrt{n} < \sqrt{130}\) is the same as \(n < 130\). I squared each given root to get an exact bound, with \(\left(\sqrt{50}\right)^2 = 50\) and \(\left(\sqrt{130}\right)^2 = 130\). Now I just need the whole numbers \(n\) with \(50 < n < 130\). Since both ends are left out, the smallest is \(51\) and the largest is \(129\). Counting from \(51\) through \(129\) gives \(129 - 51 + 1 = 79\) whole numbers. The count is \(\boxed{79}\).
review.label: r10

review.x: A number line has two posts marked on it. The left post sits at \(\sqrt{40}\) and the right post sits at \(\sqrt{210}\). A whole number \(k\) counts as "fenced in" if it lands strictly between the two posts, meaning \(\sqrt{40} < k < \sqrt{210}\). How many whole numbers \(k\) are fenced in?
hints: You do not need the exact value of either root. Find the smallest whole number whose square is bigger than 40, and the largest whole number whose square is smaller than 210.

Since \(6^2 = 36\) and \(7^2 = 49\), the root of 40 sits between 6 and 7, so the first whole number past it is 7. Since \(14^2 = 196\) and \(15^2 = 225\), the root of 210 sits between 14 and 15, so the last whole number before it is 14. Count from 7 up to 14.
review.sol: Instead of estimating the roots as decimals, compare squares, since for nonnegative numbers a bigger square means a bigger root. For the left post, \(6^2 = 36\) is less than 40 and \(7^2 = 49\) is more than 40, so \(6 < \sqrt{40} < 7\). The smallest whole number strictly above \(\sqrt{40}\) is 7. For the right post, \(14^2 = 196\) is less than 210 and \(15^2 = 225\) is more than 210, so \(14 < \sqrt{210} < 15\). The largest whole number strictly below \(\sqrt{210}\) is 14. The fenced-in whole numbers run from 7 through 14, namely 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14. Counting them gives \(14 - 7 + 1 = 8\). So \(\boxed{8}\).

review.x: Two steel beams are cut to different lengths. Beam 1 measures \(7\sqrt{5}\) meters and Beam 2 measures \(4\sqrt{15}\) meters. Neither length is a whole number, so the safest way to tell them apart is to square each one, since squaring keeps the order for positive lengths. Option 1 is Beam 1 and option 2 is Beam 2. Which beam is longer? Type 1 or 2.
hints: Both lengths are positive, so whichever one has the bigger square is the longer beam. Square each expression by squaring the number out front and squaring the root.
Beam 1 squared is \(7^2 \times 5\) and Beam 2 squared is \(4^2 \times 15\). Work out both whole numbers, then compare.
review.sol: Squaring a positive length keeps the order, so I can compare the squares instead of the messy roots. Beam 1 gives \(\left(7\sqrt{5}\right)^2 = 7^2 \times 5 = 49 \times 5 = 245\). Beam 2 gives \(\left(4\sqrt{15}\right)^2 = 4^2 \times 15 = 16 \times 15 = 240\). Since \(245 \ge 240\), Beam 1 has the larger square, so Beam 1 is the longer beam. The answer is option \(\boxed{1}\).

review.x: Here is a number that looks scary until you slow down. Take \(\sqrt{3}\), a root that never settles into a neat decimal, and raise it to the 6th power. Remember that a root squared just hands back the number underneath, so two copies of \(\sqrt{3}\) collapse into \(3\). The six factors split into three of those pairs, which means \(\sqrt{3}^6\) is the same as \(3\) multiplied by itself three times. Evaluate it. What single whole number does \(\sqrt{3}^6\) equal?
hints: Group the six copies of \(\sqrt{3}\) into pairs. Each pair \(\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{3}\) is \(\sqrt{3}^2\), which equals \(3\).
Six factors make three pairs, so you are left with \(3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3\), which is \(3^3\). Work out that product.
review.sol: An even power of a root always escapes back to a whole number, because two copies of a root undo it. Write the sixth power as three squared pieces, \(\sqrt{3}^6 = \left(\sqrt{3}^2\right)^3\). Each \(\sqrt{3}^2\) is just \(3\), since squaring a square root returns the number underneath. That leaves \(3^3 = 3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 = 27\). You can sanity check by comparing exact squares the other way, where the answer \(27\) squared is \(729\), and \(\sqrt{3}^6\) squared is \(\sqrt{3}^{12} = 3^6 = 729\) as well, so the two truly match. The irrational root vanished and a clean integer is left, \(\boxed{27}\).

review.x: Two walking trails meet at a junction. The shorter trail runs \(\sqrt{28}\) miles and the longer trail runs \(\sqrt{52}\) miles. You want to know how far you would walk if you covered both, rounded down to a whole number of miles. What is the largest whole number that is less than \(\sqrt{28} + \sqrt{52}\)? Type the single whole number.
hints: Trap each root between tenths first. Try squaring 5.2 and 5.3 to pin down \(\sqrt{28}\), then square 7.2 and 7.3 to pin down \(\sqrt{52}\).
Once you have a low estimate for each root, add the two low estimates to find a number the sum is safely above, then check that the sum still stays below 13.
review.sol: I will trap each root between tenths by squaring, since for nonnegative numbers a larger square means a larger root. For the first trail, \(5.2^2 = 27.04\) which is below \(28\), and \(5.3^2 = 28.09\) which is above \(28\), so \(5.2 < \sqrt{28} < 5.3\). For the second trail, \(7.2^2 = 51.84\) which is below \(52\), and \(7.3^2 = 53.29\) which is above \(52\), so \(7.2 < \sqrt{52} < 7.3\). Adding the low ends, the total is more than \(5.2 + 7.2 = 12.4\), and adding the high ends, the total is less than \(5.3 + 7.3 = 12.6\). So the sum sits between \(12.4\) and \(12.6\), comfortably between \(12\) and \(13\). Because \(\sqrt{28} + \sqrt{52}\) is irrational it never lands exactly on a whole number, so the largest whole number below it is \(\boxed{12}\).

blocks.x: In 8.2 you pinned an irrational root down to a number line, learning that \(\sqrt{12}\) sits a little under \(3.5\). That is a fine estimate, but it is not exact, and an estimate cannot be written cleanly or compared without fuss. This lesson does something different. It rewrites a root in a tidier exact form, no calculator and no rounding. The trick is that many numbers under a radical are secretly hiding a perfect square inside them. The number \(12\) is really \(4 \times 3\), and \(4\) is a perfect square we can root on sight. The one tool that unlocks this is a rule about how a root splits across a product, and once you have it, simplifying becomes a hunt for the biggest perfect square buried in the radicand.

Before splitting anything, let us check that a root is even allowed to split across a product. Look at \(\sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{25}\). Each piece is clean, since \(\sqrt{4} = 2\) and \(\sqrt{25} = 5\), so the product is \(2 \times 5 = 10\). Now look at the single root of the whole product, \(\sqrt{4 \times 25} = \sqrt{100}\). If the split is honest, this should land on the same value. What is \(\sqrt{100}\)?
hints: A perfect square is a number whose root is whole. Ask what whole number times itself gives \(100\).
Since \(10 \times 10 = 100\), the root of \(100\) is \(10\). Notice it matches \(\sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{25} = 2 \times 5\).
blocks.sol: Work the two sides and compare. Splitting first, \(\sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{25} = 2 \times 5 = 10\). Rooting the product, \(\sqrt{4 \times 25} = \sqrt{100} = 10\), because \(10 \times 10 = 100\). The two routes agree, which is exactly the rule we are after. A root of a product equals the product of the roots, so \(\sqrt{100} = \boxed{10}\).

blocks.x: Here is the rule that makes everything work. The square root of a product is the product of the square roots, written \(\sqrt{ab} = \sqrt{a} \times \sqrt{b}\) for nonnegative \(a\) and \(b\). So a root is allowed to split anywhere its radicand factors. That is the lever, because if one of those factors is a perfect square, its root pops out as a clean whole number while the rest stays under the radical.

Now use the rule to tidy a messy root. The number \(12\) is not a perfect square, but it factors as \(4 \times 3\), and \(4\) is a perfect square. Split the root with the product rule, \(\sqrt{12} = \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{3}\), then root the perfect-square piece. Written in simplest form, \(\sqrt{12}\) becomes a whole number times \(\sqrt{3}\). What is that whole number out in front?
hints: Split \(12\) as \(4 \times 3\), so \(\sqrt{12} = \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{3}\). The \(\sqrt{3}\) part cannot be tidied, but \(\sqrt{4}\) can.
Since \(\sqrt{4} = 2\), the root becomes \(2 \times \sqrt{3}\), written \(2\sqrt{3}\). The whole number in front is \(2\).
blocks.sol: Hunt for a perfect-square factor of \(12\). Since \(12 = 4 \times 3\) and \(4\) is a perfect square, split the root, $$\sqrt{12} = \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{3}.$$ Now \(\sqrt{4} = 2\), and \(\sqrt{3}\) has no perfect square to pull out, so it stays put. That leaves \(2 \times \sqrt{3}\), which we write \(2\sqrt{3}\). A quick check, \((2\sqrt{3})^2 = 4 \times 3 = 12\), the number we started with. The whole number in front is \(\boxed{2}\).

blocks.x: So this is the move. To simplify a square root, find the largest perfect-square factor of the number under it, take the root of that factor to pull a whole number out front, and leave whatever is left under the radical. The pulled-out number and the leftover root sit side by side, like \(2\sqrt{3}\), which means \(2\) times \(\sqrt{3}\).

blocks.cap: Simplifying \(\sqrt{72}\). The number \(72\) hides the perfect square \(36\), since \(72 = 36 \times 2\). The product rule splits the root into \(\sqrt{36} \times \sqrt{2}\), and \(\sqrt{36} = 6\) steps out front while \(\sqrt{2}\) stays under the radical, giving \(6\sqrt{2}\).

blocks.x: Try the full move yourself on \(\sqrt{72}\). The largest perfect square that divides \(72\) is \(36\), since \(72 = 36 \times 2\). Split the root, take the root of \(36\), and leave the rest under the radical. Written in simplest form as a whole number times \(\sqrt{2}\), what is the whole number in front of \(\sqrt{72}\)?
hints: Write \(72\) as \(36 \times 2\), so \(\sqrt{72} = \sqrt{36} \times \sqrt{2}\).
Since \(\sqrt{36} = 6\), the simplest form is \(6\sqrt{2}\). The number in front is \(6\).
blocks.sol: The largest perfect square dividing \(72\) is \(36\), and \(72 = 36 \times 2\). Split the root, $$\sqrt{72} = \sqrt{36} \times \sqrt{2} = 6\sqrt{2}.$$ The \(\sqrt{2}\) cannot be reduced further, so \(6\sqrt{2}\) is simplest. Check, \((6\sqrt{2})^2 = 36 \times 2 = 72\). The whole number in front is \(\boxed{6}\).

blocks.x: Sometimes the question is about what is left behind, not what comes out. Simplify \(\sqrt{48}\). The largest perfect square dividing \(48\) is \(16\), since \(48 = 16 \times 3\). Once you pull the whole number out front, a single square root remains. What number is left under the radical in the simplest form of \(\sqrt{48}\)?
hints: Write \(48 = 16 \times 3\), so \(\sqrt{48} = \sqrt{16} \times \sqrt{3}\). Which piece stays under the root?
Since \(\sqrt{16} = 4\), the simplest form is \(4\sqrt{3}\). The number left under the radical is \(3\).
blocks.sol: Split off the largest perfect-square factor. Since \(48 = 16 \times 3\), $$\sqrt{48} = \sqrt{16} \times \sqrt{3} = 4\sqrt{3}.$$ The \(4\) comes out front and the \(3\) stays under the radical, because \(3\) has no perfect-square factor bigger than \(1\). So the number left under the radical is \(\boxed{3}\).

blocks.x: Simplify \(\sqrt{98}\). Look for the largest perfect square hiding in \(98\). Since \(98 = 49 \times 2\) and \(49\) is a perfect square, the root tidies into a whole number times \(\sqrt{2}\). What is the whole number in front?
hints: The perfect squares to test are \(4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49\). Which one divides \(98\)? Try \(49\).
Since \(98 = 49 \times 2\), we get \(\sqrt{98} = \sqrt{49} \times \sqrt{2} = 7\sqrt{2}\). The number in front is \(7\).
blocks.sol: Search for a perfect-square factor of \(98\). Testing \(49\), we find \(98 = 49 \times 2\), so $$\sqrt{98} = \sqrt{49} \times \sqrt{2} = 7\sqrt{2}.$$ The leftover \(2\) is square-free, so \(7\sqrt{2}\) is simplest. Check, \((7\sqrt{2})^2 = 49 \times 2 = 98\). The whole number in front is \(\boxed{7}\).

blocks.x: A square root is in simplest form when the number left under the radical has no perfect-square factor bigger than \(1\). At that point nothing more can be pulled out. If you ever pull out a small square and the leftover still hides another square, you are not done yet, so reaching for the largest perfect-square factor right away finishes the job in a single step.

Not every root simplifies. Consider \(\sqrt{30}\). Factor \(30\) all the way down to \(2 \times 3 \times 5\). Every factor appears only once, so no factor is repeated to form a perfect square. To decide whether \(\sqrt{30}\) can be simplified at all, find the largest perfect square that divides \(30\). What is it?
hints: A perfect square needs a repeated factor, like \(2 \times 2\) or \(3 \times 3\). In \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\), is any factor repeated?
None of \(4, 9, 16, 25\) divides \(30\). The only perfect square that does is \(1\), so \(\sqrt{30}\) is already in simplest form.
blocks.sol: Factor \(30\) completely, \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\). A perfect-square factor needs a repeated prime, but here every prime shows up only once, so none of \(4\), \(9\), \(16\), or \(25\) divides \(30\). The largest perfect square dividing \(30\) is just \(1\). Since pulling out \(\sqrt{1} = 1\) changes nothing, \(\sqrt{30}\) is already in simplest form. The largest perfect-square factor is \(\boxed{1}\).

blocks.x: The product rule also works in reverse, joining two roots into one before you simplify. Take \(\sqrt{10} \times \sqrt{5}\). First combine them under a single radical with the rule, \(\sqrt{10} \times \sqrt{5} = \sqrt{10 \times 5} = \sqrt{50}\). Now simplify \(\sqrt{50}\), which hides the perfect square \(25\). Written as a whole number times \(\sqrt{2}\), what is the whole number in front?
hints: Combine first, \(\sqrt{10} \times \sqrt{5} = \sqrt{50}\). Then look for the largest perfect square dividing \(50\).
Since \(50 = 25 \times 2\), we get \(\sqrt{50} = \sqrt{25} \times \sqrt{2} = 5\sqrt{2}\). The number in front is \(5\).
blocks.sol: Join the roots with the product rule, $$\sqrt{10} \times \sqrt{5} = \sqrt{10 \times 5} = \sqrt{50}.$$ Now simplify. The largest perfect square dividing \(50\) is \(25\), and \(50 = 25 \times 2\), so $$\sqrt{50} = \sqrt{25} \times \sqrt{2} = 5\sqrt{2}.$$ The whole number in front is \(\boxed{5}\).

blocks.cap: Two routes for \(\sqrt{288}\). Pull out only the small square \(4\) and you get \(2\sqrt{72}\), which is not finished because \(72\) still hides the square \(36\). Pull out the largest square \(144\) at once and you land on \(12\sqrt{2}\) in a single step. Both reach the same simplest form, but the largest factor gets there faster.

blocks.x: For centuries an irrational root like \(\sqrt{2}\) was called a surd, a word that traveled a long way. Arabic scholars translating Greek mathematics described such a root as deaf or mute, since it could not be spoken as a clean ratio, and the Latin word for deaf, surdus, stuck to it. The bar drawn across the top of a radical, the part that tells you exactly which numbers sit under the root, is called a vinculum, Latin for a bond or tie. So a tidy expression like \(2\sqrt{3}\) carries two old ideas at once, a number that once seemed unspeakable and a little tie holding its radicand together.

Time for the trickiest one, where spotting the largest square saves real work. Simplify \(\sqrt{507}\). It looks stubborn, but \(507 = 169 \times 3\), and \(169\) is a perfect square. Pull it out to write \(\sqrt{507}\) as a whole number times \(\sqrt{3}\). What is the whole number in front?
hints: The number \(507\) is odd, so \(4, 16, 36, 64, 100\) cannot divide it. Try odd perfect squares like \(9\), \(25\), \(49\), \(121\), \(169\).
Since \(507 = 169 \times 3\) and \(\sqrt{169} = 13\), the simplest form is \(13\sqrt{3}\). The number in front is \(13\).
blocks.sol: Search \(507\) for a perfect-square factor. It is odd, so skip the even squares, and testing odd ones gives \(507 = 169 \times 3\), with \(169 = 13^2\). Split the root, $$\sqrt{507} = \sqrt{169} \times \sqrt{3} = 13\sqrt{3}.$$ The leftover \(3\) is square-free, so \(13\sqrt{3}\) is simplest. Check, \((13\sqrt{3})^2 = 169 \times 3 = 507\). The whole number in front is \(\boxed{13}\).

review.x: Simplify \(\sqrt{8}\). The largest perfect square dividing \(8\) is \(4\), since \(8 = 4 \times 2\). Written as a whole number times \(\sqrt{2}\), what is the whole number in front?
hints: Write \(8 = 4 \times 2\), so \(\sqrt{8} = \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{2}\).
Since \(\sqrt{4} = 2\), the simplest form is \(2\sqrt{2}\). The number in front is \(2\).
review.sol: Since \(8 = 4 \times 2\), $$\sqrt{8} = \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{2} = 2\sqrt{2}.$$ The whole number in front is \(\boxed{2}\).

review.x: Simplify \(\sqrt{75}\). Since \(75 = 25 \times 3\) and \(25\) is a perfect square, the root tidies into a whole number times \(\sqrt{3}\). What is the whole number in front?
hints: Write \(75 = 25 \times 3\), so \(\sqrt{75} = \sqrt{25} \times \sqrt{3}\).
Since \(\sqrt{25} = 5\), the simplest form is \(5\sqrt{3}\). The number in front is \(5\).
review.sol: Since \(75 = 25 \times 3\), $$\sqrt{75} = \sqrt{25} \times \sqrt{3} = 5\sqrt{3}.$$ The whole number in front is \(\boxed{5}\).

review.x: Simplify \(\sqrt{44}\). The largest perfect square dividing \(44\) is \(4\), since \(44 = 4 \times 11\). After you pull the whole number out front, one square root remains. What number is left under the radical?
hints: Write \(44 = 4 \times 11\), so \(\sqrt{44} = \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{11}\).
Since \(\sqrt{4} = 2\), the simplest form is \(2\sqrt{11}\). The number left under the radical is \(11\).
review.sol: Since \(44 = 4 \times 11\), $$\sqrt{44} = \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{11} = 2\sqrt{11}.$$ The \(11\) is square-free and stays under the radical, so the number left under it is \(\boxed{11}\).

review.x: Simplify \(\sqrt{128}\). The largest perfect square dividing \(128\) is \(64\), since \(128 = 64 \times 2\). Written as a whole number times \(\sqrt{2}\), what is the whole number in front?
hints: Write \(128 = 64 \times 2\), so \(\sqrt{128} = \sqrt{64} \times \sqrt{2}\).
Since \(\sqrt{64} = 8\), the simplest form is \(8\sqrt{2}\). The number in front is \(8\).
review.sol: Since \(128 = 64 \times 2\), $$\sqrt{128} = \sqrt{64} \times \sqrt{2} = 8\sqrt{2}.$$ The whole number in front is \(\boxed{8}\).

review.x: Use the product rule, then simplify. Combine \(\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{12}\) into a single root, then evaluate it. Since \(\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{12} = \sqrt{3 \times 12} = \sqrt{36}\), this one comes out to a whole number. What is that whole number?
hints: Combine, \(\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{12} = \sqrt{36}\).
Since \(36\) is a perfect square, \(\sqrt{36} = 6\), with no radical left at all.
review.sol: Join the roots, \(\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{12} = \sqrt{3 \times 12} = \sqrt{36}\). Since \(36\) is a perfect square, \(\sqrt{36} = 6\), a clean whole number. So the value is \(\boxed{6}\).

review.x: To simplify \(\sqrt{60}\), you first need its largest perfect-square factor. Factor \(60 = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 5\) and gather the repeated prime into a square. What is the largest perfect square that divides \(60\)?
hints: A perfect-square factor comes from a repeated prime. In \(60 = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 5\), the \(2\) repeats.
The repeated \(2 \times 2 = 4\) is a perfect square, and no larger one divides \(60\), so the answer is \(4\).
review.sol: Factor \(60 = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 5\). The only repeated prime is \(2\), giving the perfect square \(2 \times 2 = 4\). No larger perfect square divides \(60\), since \(3\) and \(5\) appear only once. So the largest perfect-square factor is \(\boxed{4}\). (It would simplify \(\sqrt{60}\) to \(2\sqrt{15}\).)

review.x: Decide whether \(\sqrt{33}\) can be simplified. Factor \(33\) completely, then find the largest perfect square that divides it. What is that largest perfect square?
hints: Factor \(33 = 3 \times 11\). Is any prime repeated?
No prime repeats, so no perfect square bigger than \(1\) divides \(33\). It is already in simplest form.
review.sol: Factor \(33 = 3 \times 11\). Each prime appears once, so there is no repeated factor to form a perfect square. The largest perfect square dividing \(33\) is \(\boxed{1}\), which means \(\sqrt{33}\) is already in simplest form.

review.x: Use the product rule, then simplify. Combine \(\sqrt{15} \times \sqrt{6}\) into one root, then write it in simplest form. Since \(\sqrt{15} \times \sqrt{6} = \sqrt{90}\) and \(90 = 9 \times 10\), the result is a whole number times \(\sqrt{10}\). What is the whole number in front?
hints: Combine, \(\sqrt{15} \times \sqrt{6} = \sqrt{90}\). The largest perfect square dividing \(90\) is \(9\).
Since \(90 = 9 \times 10\), \(\sqrt{90} = \sqrt{9} \times \sqrt{10} = 3\sqrt{10}\). The number in front is \(3\).
review.sol: Join the roots, \(\sqrt{15} \times \sqrt{6} = \sqrt{15 \times 6} = \sqrt{90}\). The largest perfect square dividing \(90\) is \(9\), and \(90 = 9 \times 10\), so $$\sqrt{90} = \sqrt{9} \times \sqrt{10} = 3\sqrt{10}.$$ The whole number in front is \(\boxed{3}\).

review.x: Here is the trickiest one. Simplify \(\sqrt{675}\) by finding its largest perfect-square factor. Since \(675 = 225 \times 3\) and \(225\) is a perfect square, the root becomes a whole number times \(\sqrt{3}\). What is the whole number in front?
hints: The number \(675\) is odd, so test odd perfect squares like \(9\), \(25\), \(225\). Try \(225\).
Since \(675 = 225 \times 3\) and \(\sqrt{225} = 15\), the simplest form is \(15\sqrt{3}\). The number in front is \(15\).
review.sol: Search \(675\) for its largest perfect-square factor. It is odd, and \(675 = 225 \times 3\) with \(225 = 15^2\). Split the root, $$\sqrt{675} = \sqrt{225} \times \sqrt{3} = 15\sqrt{3}.$$ Check, \((15\sqrt{3})^2 = 225 \times 3 = 675\). The whole number in front is \(\boxed{15}\).
